elements.sent-2411-001: We model a spin-phase transition in a two-dimensional square array, or a lateral superlattice, of quantum rings in an external perpendicular homogeneous magnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-002: The electron system is placed in a circular cylindrical far-infrared photon cavity with a single circularly symmetric photon mode.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our numerical results reveal that the spin ordering of the two-dimensional electron gas in each quantum ring can be influenced or controlled by the electron-photon coupling strength and the energy of the photons.
elements.sent-2411-004: The Coulomb interaction between the electrons is described by a spin-density functional approach, but the para- and the diamagnetic electron-photon interactions are modeled via a configuration interaction formalism in a truncated many-body Fock-space, which is updated in each iteration step of the density functional approach.
elements.sent-2411-005: In the absence of external electromagnetic pulses this spin-phase transition is replicated in the orbital magnetization of the rings.
elements.sent-2411-006: The spin-phase transition can be suppressed by a strong electron-photon interaction.
elements.sent-2411-007: In addition, fluctuations in the spin configuration are found in dynamical calculations, where the system is excited by a time-dependent scheme specially fit for emphasizing the diamagnetic electron-photon interaction.
elements.sent-2411-008: Researchers have been exploring and modeling \cite{Flick2017,FlickRiveraNarang+2018+1479+1501,10.1063/1.5142502,Buchholz2019 the effects of placing chemical systems \cite{https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201107033 , molecules \cite{Schafer2021ShiningLO , and nanoscale electron systems in photon cavities \cite{PhysRevB.101.075301,PhysRevB.101.205140 in order to modify or control their properties.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: On the theoretical side, several methods have been used, some being gathered under the umbrella concept of quantum electrodynamical density functional theory (QEDFT) \cite{doi:10.1021/acsphotonics.7b01279,flick2021simple,10.1063/5.0123909,PhysRevLett.133.096401 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Concurrently, this interest has been extended to large systems with two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) in heterostructures.
elements.sent-2411-011: Experiments have indicated that the 2DEG in GaAs heterostructures is especially advantageous for fundamental research on the 2DEG-cavity photon interactions due to its high purity and polarizability \cite{Zhang1005:2016,doi:10.1126/science.1216022 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The free homogeneous 2DEG placed in a parallel plate cavity has been modeled \cite{PhysRevResearch.4.013012,PhysRevLett.123.047202,PhysRevB.105.205424 , as well as the 2DEG modulated in a superlattice and subjected to the static electronic Coulomb interaction, both in the static \cite{PhysRevB.106.115308,mughnetsyan2023magnetic,PhysRevB.109.235306 , or in the dynamic case after short excitation pulse \cite{PhysRevB.108.115306,PhysRevB.110.205301 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Previously, the present group of researchers has modeled the modulated 2DEG in a square array of quantum dots interacting with the quantized TE$_{011}$ mode of a circular cylindrical cavity.
elements.sent-2411-014: That was done in order to strengthen the diamagnetic electron-photon interaction of the system placed in a homogeneous external magnetic field promoting persistent circular or rotational currents.
elements.sent-2411-015: The model calculations were accomplished for the static system using the quantum electrodynamic density functional theory tensor product (QED-DFT-TP) formalism presented by Malave {\it et al.}\ \cite{10.1063/5.0123909 adapted to the 2DEG and the external magnetic field \cite{PhysRevB.109.235306 .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Particularly, the excitations of the system with a short pulse-formed modulation of the electron-photon interaction in a self-consistent real-time approach showed how diamagnetic two-photon transitions are strongly promoted in the system \cite{PhysRevB.110.205301 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: This has directed us to consider a system with the 2DEG in a superlattice of quantum rings.
elements.sent-2411-018: There are two reasons for the present study: i) the ring array may be more susceptible to the symmetry of the TE$_{011}$ mode of a cylindrical cavity, and ii) the exchange correlations between the rings may play a special role in the system.
elements.sent-2411-019: This last point is supported by our earlier findings for the dot system, i.e.\ an array of quantum dots, where the results of the calculations using the QED-DFT-TP \cite{PhysRevB.109.235306 , or the QEDFT \cite{PhysRevB.106.115308 clearly show that, an increased electron-photon interaction weakens the Coulomb exchange interactions considerably.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Last but not least, we believe that a quantum ring array is a suitable platform to further test the QED-DFT-TP method.
elements.sent-2411-021: The paper is organized as follows: In Sec.\ \ref{Model we briefly describe the model, which was already introduced in our previous works.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The results and discussion thereof are found in Sec.\ \ref{Results , with the conclusions drawn in Sec.\ \ref{Conclusions .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Appendix \ref{Tech-details contains technical details of the methodology used for the modeling.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The mutual Coulomb interaction of the electrons in the 2DEG are described within spin density functional theory using an exact direct term, and approximate exchange correlations terms detailed in the Appendix of Ref.\ \cite{PhysRevB.106.115308 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: The extension to systems in homogeneous external magnetic field was presented by Lubin {\it et al.}\ and Koskinen {\it et al.}\ \cite{Lubin97:10373,Koskinen97:1389 and built on development by Barth and Hedin \cite{Barth_1972 , and Tanatar and Ceperley \cite{Tanatar89:5005 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: The 2DEG in the $xy$-plane is in an external homogeneous magnetic field $\bm{B = B{\bm{e _z}$ described by the vector potential $\bm{A = (B/2)(-y,x)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The external magnetic field introduces the natural length scale, the magnetic length $l = (\hbar c/(eB))^{1/2}$, and an energy scale, the cyclotron energy $\hbar\omega_c = (eB/(m^*c))$.
elements.sent-2411-028: The potential defining the square array, or lateral superlattice, of rings is \begin{equation}V_per(r) = -V_0\left[\sin \left(\frac{g_1x}{2} \right) \sin\left(\frac{g_2y}{2}\right) \right]^2 \nonumber +V_0\left[\sin \left(\frac{g_1x}{2} \right) \sin\left(\frac{g_2y}{2}\right) \right]^4, \label{Vper}\end{equation} with $V_0 = 64.0$ meV.
elements.sent-2411-029: The potential is displayed in Fig.\ \ref{Vxy for 4 unit cells of the extended superlattice hosting the 2DEG.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig01.pdf} \caption{Four unit cells of the potentials defining the two-dimensional array of quantum rings. The superlattice length $L = 100$ nm.} \label{Vxy}
elements.sent-2411-030: The square superlattice of quantum rings is spanned by the spatial vectors $\bm{R =n\bm{l _1+m\bm{l _2$ with $n,m\in \bm{Z}$, where the unit vectors are $\bm{l _1 = L\bm{e _x$ and $\bm{l _2 = L\bm{e _y$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The reciprocal lattice is spanned by $\bm{G = G_1\bm{g _1 + G_2\bm{g _2$ with $G_1, G_2\in \mathbf{Z}$ and its unit vectors $\bm{g _1 = 2\pi\bm{e _x/L$ and $\bm{g _2 = 2\pi\bm{e _y/L$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The period of the superlattice is $L = 100$ nm, setting another length scale competing with the magnetic length $l$ \cite{Hofstadter76:2239 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: The 2DEG is placed in the center of a circular cylindrical FIR photon cavity.
elements.sent-2411-034: The long wavelength approximation for the vector potential for the TE$_{011}$ cavity photon mode was derived in the Appendix of Ref.\ \cite{PhysRevB.109.235306 to be \begin{equation}{\bm A}_\gamma (\bm{r}) = \bm{e}_\phi {\cal A}_\gamma\left(a^\dagger_\gamma + a_\gamma \right) \left(\frac{r}{l} \right), \label{Agamma}\end{equation} where $\bm{e _\phi$ is the unit angular vector for the polar coordinates, $(r,\phi)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The vector potential (\ref{Agamma ) exhibits the same spatial form as the vector potential ${\bm A}$ of the external homogeneous magnetic field $\bm{ B = B\bm{e _z$ \cite{PhysRevB.109.235306 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The electrons of the 2DEG interact with the cavity photon mode according to \begin{equation}H_int = \frac{1}{c}\int_{R^2} dr\; {\bm J}({\bm r})\cdot{\bm A}_\gamma (r) \nonumber + \frac{e^2}{2m^*c}\int_{R^2} dr\; n_e(r)A^2_\gamma(r), \label{e-g}\end{equation} where both the para- and the diamagnetic parts of the electron-photon interaction are accounted for.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}H_int = \frac{1}{c}\int_{R^2} dr\; {\bm J}({\bm r})\cdot{\bm A}_\gamma (r) \nonumber + \frac{e^2}{2m^*c}\int_{R^2} dr\; n_e(r)A^2_\gamma(r), \label{e-g}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-037: In the static part of the calculations the electron and the current densities are defined as \begin{equation}n_e(r) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^2}\sum_{\alpha\sigma} \int^{\pi}_{-\pi}d\theta\; \left|\psi_{\alpha\theta\sigma}(r) \right|^2 f(E_{\alpha\theta\sigma}-\mu), \label{ne}\end{equation} \begin{equation}J_{i}(r) = \frac{-e}{m^*(2\pi)^2}\sum_{{\bm \alpha}\sigma}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} d\theta\; \Re \left\{ \psi_{\alpha\theta\sigma}^*(r)\pi_i \psi_{\alpha\theta\sigma}(r) \right\}\nonumber f(E_{\alpha\theta\sigma}-\mu), \label{currD}\end{equation} respectively, for $i=x$ or $y$.
elements.sent-2411-038: In the Appendix of Ref.\ \cite{PhysRevB.109.235306 the electron-photon interaction (\ref{e-g ) is rewritten in terms of the photon creation and annihilation operators as \begin{equation}\label{e-gIxIyN} H_int = g_\gamma \hbar\omega_c \left\{ lI_x + lI_y\right\} \left(a^\dagger_\gamma + a_\gamma\right) + g^2_\gamma \hbar\omega_c {\cal N}\left\{\left(a^\dagger_\gamma a_\gamma + \frac{1}{2}\right) +\frac{1}{2}\left(a^\dagger_\gamma a^\dagger_\gamma + a_\gamma a_\gamma\right)\right\}\nonumber,\end{equation} where conveniently and importantly, the constants $I_x$, $I_y$, and ${\cal N}$ turn out as functionals the electron charge- and current densities.}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{e-gIxIyN} H_int = g_\gamma \hbar\omega_c \left\{ lI_x + lI_y\right\} \left(a^\dagger_\gamma + a_\gamma\right) + g^2_\gamma \hbar\omega_c {\cal N}\left\{\left(a^\dagger_\gamma a_\gamma + \frac{1}{2}\right) +\frac{1}{2}\left(a^\dagger_\gamma a^\dagger_\gamma + a_\gamma a_\gamma\right)\right\}\nonumber,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-039: Previous calculations with the model for a square array of quantum dots interacting with the TE$_{011}$ cylindrical mode show the importance of retaining all the terms of the diamagnetic part of the interaction, the second line in Eq.\ (\ref{e-gIxIyN ) \cite{PhysRevB.110.205301 .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The dimensionless electron-photon coupling constant emerging in the derivation is \begin{equation}g_\gamma = \left\{ \left( \frac{e{\cal A}_\gamma}{c} \right) \frac{l}{\hbar} \right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-041: The energy of the photons in the single cavity mode is $E_\gamma = \hbar\omega_\gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-042: In order to explore the dynamical evolution of the system, it is excited with a short pulse-like modulation of the electron-photon interactions. \begin{equation}\label{Ht} H_ext(t) = F(t) \biggl[ g_\gamma \hbar\omega_c\left\{lI_x + lI_y \right\} \left( a^\dagger_\gamma + a_\gamma \right) + \left. g_\gamma^2 \hbar\omega_c {\cal N} \left\{ \left( a^\dagger_\gamma a_\gamma + \frac{1}{2}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\left( a^\dagger_\gamma a^\dagger_\gamma + a_\gamma a_\gamma \right) \right\} \right]\nonumber\end{equation} with \begin{equation}F(t) = \left( \frac{V_t}{\hbar\omega_c}\right) (\Gamma t)^2 \exp{(-\Gamma t)}\cos{(\omega_\mathrm{ext} t)}. \label{ft}\end{equation} The time-evolution of the system is calculated self-consistently with the Liouville-von Neumann equation for the density, or the probability, operator $\rho^{\bm{\theta (t)$ for each $\bm{\theta}$-point in the first Brillouin zone of the reciprocal lattice. \begin{equation}i\hbar\partial_t \rho^{\bm{\theta}} (t) = \left[ H[\rho^{\bm{\theta}}(t)], \rho^{\bm{\theta}}(t)\right]. \label{L-vN}\end{equation} That is possible as, neither any term in the electron-photon interaction (\ref{e-gIxIyN ), nor the excitation (\ref{Ht ) couple different $\bm{\theta}$ points in the reciprocal lattice.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\label{Ht} H_ext(t) = F(t) \biggl[ g_\gamma \hbar\omega_c\left\{lI_x + lI_y \right\} \left( a^\dagger_\gamma + a_\gamma \right) + \left. g_\gamma^2 \hbar\omega_c {\cal N} \left\{ \left( a^\dagger_\gamma a_\gamma + \frac{1}{2}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\left( a^\dagger_\gamma a^\dagger_\gamma + a_\gamma a_\gamma \right) \right\} \right]\nonumber\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-043: Self-consistency refers to that the direct Coulomb and the exchange-correlation functionals, depending on the electron density, have to be updated in each iteration within each time step together with the functionals $I_x$, $I_y$, and ${\cal N}$ of the electron charge- and current densities.
elements.sent-2411-044: The total time-dependent Hamiltonian $H[\rho^{\bm{\theta (t)]$ is a functional of the time-dependent density operators $\rho^{\bm{\theta (t)$ and it is also an explicit function of time through (\ref{ft ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: It includes the periodic ring potential (\ref{Vper ), the electron-photon interaction (\ref{e-gIxIyN ), the electron Coulomb direct and exchange-correlation terms, the Zeeman spin energy term, the time-dependent modulation of the electron-photon interaction (\ref{Ht -\ref{ft ), and the electron and photon ``kinetic terms'' \cite{PhysRevB.110.205301 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Subsequently, the time-dependent electron and current densities have to be calculated from the density operator instead of the Fermi distribution as the excitation throws the system out of equilibrium \cite{PhysRevB.110.205301 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: For the dynamic system we calculate the time-dependent mean photon number \begin{equation}N_\gamma (t) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^2}\sum_{\sigma}\int^{\pi}_{-\pi} d\bm{\theta}\; \mathrm{Tr} \left\{ \rho^{\bm{\theta}}_{\sigma}(t) a^\dagger_\gamma a_\gamma\right\} \label{Ngt}\end{equation} and the time-dependent mean orbital magnetization \begin{equation}M_o(t) = \frac{1}{2c{\cal A}}\int_{\cal A} d\bm{r} \left( {\bf r}\times \langle {\bf J}({\bf r},t) \rangle \right) \cdot{\bm{e}_z}. \label{Mo}\end{equation} with ${\cal A = L^2$.}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}M_o(t) = \frac{1}{2c{\cal A}}\int_{\cal A} dr \left( {\bf r}\times \langle {\bf J}({\bf r},t) \rangle \right) \cdot{e_z}. \label{Mo}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-048: In previous publications we have used the notation $Q_J$ for the orbital magnetization to emphasize its connection to a moment of the current density.
elements.sent-2411-049: In addition we evaluate the time-dependent spin magnetization \begin{equation}M_s(t) = -\frac{g\mu^*_\mathrm{B}}{\cal A}\int_{\cal A} d\bm{r} \langle \sigma_z (\bm{r},t)\rangle , \label{Ms}\end{equation} with $\sigma_z$ identified as $\zeta = (n_\uparrow - n_\downarrow)/n_\mathrm{e}$, the spin polarization defined in the spin-DFT formalism.
elements.sent-2411-050: The integrand in Eq.\ (\ref{Ngt ) can be used to calculate the photon density in the first Brillouin zone of the reciprocal space \begin{equation}n_\gamma (\theta_1,\theta_2) = \langle a_\gamma^\dagger a_\gamma \rangle^{\bm{\theta}} \label{ngdist}\end{equation} for the static or the dynamical system.}
elements.sent-2411-051: In the dynamical case the average, or the mean value, is taken with respect to the density operators as in Eq.\ (\ref{Ngt ) which can be reduced to diagonal operators in the static system, see Ref.\ \cite{PhysRevB.109.235306 .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: For the model calculations, we assume GaAs parameters: the effective electron mass $m^* = 0.067m_e$, the dielectric constant $\kappa = 12.4$, and the effective $g$-factor $g^* = -0.44$.
elements.sent-2411-053: The magnetic flux through a unit cell comes from the commensurability condition $B{\cal A = BL^2 =pq\Phi_0$ with the integers $p$ and $q$ \cite{Ferrari90:4598,Silberbauer92:7355,Gudmundsson95:16744 , and the flux quantum $\Phi_0 = hc/e$.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: For the excitation pulse (\ref{ft ) we use $\hbar\omega_\mathrm{ext = 3.5$ meV, $\hbar\Gamma = 0.5$ meV, and $V_t/\hbar\omega_c = 0.8$.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The total energy $E_{tot}$ increases with increasing electron-photon coupling $g_\gamma$ (a) and the same can be said about the photon number in each ring $N_\gamma$ (b).
elements.sent-2411-056: There is a more subtle change in the orbital magnetization $M_o$ (c) at low photon energy $E_\gamma$ due to the varying occupation of the first photon replica.
elements.sent-2411-057: Importantly, we notice almost no change in the spin-configuration, seen through the spin magnetization (d), but a comparison of the results for the array of rings to the results for an array of dots for the same lattice size, $L = 100$ nm, makes immediately clear one fundamental difference.
elements.sent-2411-058: In the array of rings the Coulomb exchange interaction is stronger than for the dot array.
elements.sent-2411-059: It is sufficient to look at Fig.\ 2(d) in Ref.\ \cite{PhysRevB.109.235306 showing the spin magnetization for $N_\mathrm{e = 2$ at the magnetic flux $pq = 2$, corresponding to the magnetic field $B = 0.827$ T.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: It is very close to 0 for photon energies 1.0 and 2.0 meV and the dimensionless electron-photon coupling $g_\gamma$ in the range from almost 0 to 0.3.
elements.sent-2411-061: Therefore, the spin configuration of each dot is a singlet state.
elements.sent-2411-062: For individual dots it has been known for a long time that the singlet state preferred by the system at low magnetic field changes to a triplet as the magnetic field is increased \cite{Pfannkuche93:2244 .}
elements.sent-2411-063: Note though, that in the calculations for a single dot a positive background charge is usually not assumed, so the direct Coulomb interaction is stronger than in an array, where a positive background charge is assumed.
elements.sent-2411-064: Figure \ref{Yfirlit-pq2-Ne02 (in the present paper) shows the total energy $E_\gamma$, the mean photon number $N_\gamma$, the orbital $M_o$, and the spin magnetization $M_s$ per unit cell for $pq = 2$ and $N_\mathrm{e = 2$.}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig02a.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig02b.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig02c.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig02d.pdf} \caption{The total energy $E_\mathrm{tot}$ (a), the mean number of photons $N_\gamma$ (b), the orbital magnetization $M_o$ (c), and the spin magnetization $M_s$ (d) of the 2DEG in a unit cell as function of the photon energy $E_\gamma$ for different values of the dimensionless electron-photon coupling $g_\gamma$. $T = 1.0$ K, $N_\mathrm{e} = 2$, $pq = 2$, and $M_0 = \mu^*_\mathrm{B}/L^2$.} \label{Yfirlit-pq2-Ne02}
elements.sent-2411-065: The spin magnetization in Fig.\ \ref{Yfirlit-pq2-Ne02 (d) shows that the two electrons in each ring are in a spin triplet state, even for very high electron-photon coupling.}
elements.sent-2411-066: We find the same happening for the lower magnetic flux $pq = 1$ (not shown here) for the array of rings, the electrons remain in the triplet spin state.
elements.sent-2411-067: For the dot system, i.e.\ an array of quantum dots, all our previous calculations using the QED-DFT-TP \cite{PhysRevB.109.235306 , or the QEDFT \cite{PhysRevB.106.115308 show clearly that within the parameter range used here, the increased electron-photon interaction weakens the Coulomb exchange interactions considerably.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Below, we check better the static and dynamic properties of the ring array with $N_\mathrm{e = 2$ and $pq = 2$ flux quanta flowing through the unit cell before analyzing the situation for $N_\mathrm{e = 3$.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: After the same dynamical excitation (\ref{Ht ) as is used in Ref.\ \cite{PhysRevB.110.205301 the mean photon number oscillates as is displayed in Fig.\ \ref{Ng-pq2-Ne02 for $g_\gamma = 0.08$ in (a), and $0.10$ in (b).}}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig03a.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig03b.pdf} \caption{The dynamic mean photon number $N_\gamma (t)$ per unit cell of the 2DEG as a function of time for the first 30 ps for different values of the photon energy $E_\gamma$ and $g_\gamma = 0.08$ (a), and the electron-photon coupling $g_\gamma = 0.10$ (b). $N_\mathrm{e} = 2$, $pq = 2$.} \label{Ng-pq2-Ne02}
elements.sent-2411-070: The oscillations are similar as for the quantum dot array, except for the observation that the excitation of the ring array looks to be more effective.
elements.sent-2411-071: That is not surprising as the active states of the ring system, in a polar coordinate system not used here, have mainly contributions from the eigenstates of the angular momentum operator for finite angular momentum, $M\neq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-072: In a quantum dot the ground state has a higher contribution from a state with zero angular momentum, the $M=0$ state, in polar coordinates, that is less susceptible to a circular symmetric excitation.
elements.sent-2411-073: In polar coordinates $M$ is the angular momentum quantum number.
elements.sent-2411-074: The Fourier power spectra for the two-electron rings are displayed in Fig.\ \ref{P-pq2-Ne02 .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig04a.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig04b.pdf} \caption{The Fourier power spectra of the mean photon number per unit cell $N_\gamma (\omega )$ for different values of the photon energy $E_\gamma$ and the electron-photon coupling $g_\gamma = 0.08$ (a), and $g_\gamma = 0.10$ (b). $N_\mathrm{e} = 2$, $pq = 2$, and $\hbar\omega_c = 1.429$ meV.} \label{P-pq2-Ne02}

elements.sent-2411-077: This interaction shift is exactly the reason for which excitation peak is best excited by the frequency spectrum the excitation forces on the system \cite{PhysRevB.110.205301 .}
elements.sent-2411-078: The spectrum of the excitation is the same for Fig.\ \ref{P-pq2-Ne02 (a) and (b).}
elements.sent-2411-079: First, in Fig.\ \ref{d-pq2-Ne03 we observe through the electron density (a) and the photon induced change of it (b), that we are dealing with a ring formed electron system in each unit cell of the superlattice.}
elements.sent-2411-080: In addition, we see the distribution of the photons in the first Brillouin zone of the reciprocal space (c).
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth,bb= 20 60 370 265]{Fig05a.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth,bb= 20 60 370 265]{Fig05b.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth,bb= 20 60 370 265]{Fig05c.pdf} \vspace*{0.8cm} \caption{The electron density $n_\mathrm{e}l^2$ (a), the change in the electron density $\Delta n_\mathrm{e}l^2 = [n_\mathrm{e}(g_\gamma = 0.10) - n_\mathrm{e}(g_\gamma = 0.01)]l^2$ (b), and the mean photon distribution $n_\gamma (\theta_1,\theta_2)$ in the reciprocal unit cell (c), see Eq.\ (\ref{ngdist}). $g_\gamma = 0.10$, $N_\mathrm{e} = 3$, $pq = 2$, $E_\gamma = 0.70$ meV, $l = 28.21$ nm.} \label{d-pq2-Ne03}
elements.sent-2411-081: The same is true for two electrons at $pq = 2$.
elements.sent-2411-082: The ring structure is not as clear for 2 or 3 electrons at the lower magnetic flux $pq = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-083: There, the hole in the center of the charge distribution is not quite as deep as for $pq = 2$.
elements.sent-2411-084: That can be explained by the magnetic length $l$, that is $28.21$ nm at $pq = 2$, but $39.89$ at $pq = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-085: We notice in Fig.\ \ref{d-pq2-Ne03 (b) that the electron density is polarized radially outwards as the electron-photon coupling is increased.}
elements.sent-2411-086: This is in accordance with our results for dot arrays, both in the QEDFT \cite{PhysRevB.106.115308 and the QED-DFT-TP \cite{PhysRevB.109.235306 formalism.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Of course, the symmetry of the square array can distort the charge polarization, we see a slight indication of that here.
elements.sent-2411-088: The electron density seen in Fig.\ \ref{d-pq2-Ne03 suggests that the inter-ring Coulomb exchange is strengthened for an array of rings due to the ``closer spacing'' of rings than dots for few electrons, and interestingly, increased electron-photon coupling slightly reduces the effective distance between the rings, if it is measured as the distance between the maxima in the charge distribution of the rings.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Figure \ref{Yfirlit-pq2-Ne03 shows the total energy $E_\mathrm{tot}$, the mean photon number $N_\gamma$, the orbital $M_o$, and the spin magnetization $M_s$ per unit cell for $pq = 2$ and $N_\mathrm{e = 3$.}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Figure \ref{Yfirlit-pq2-Ne03 should be compared to Fig.\ \ref{Yfirlit-pq2-Ne02 for two electrons in each ring.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: We observe a clear phase change from the fully spin polarized state to a phase of single unpaired spins in each ring, when either the photon energy $E_\gamma$, or the electron-photon coupling $g_\gamma$ is increased for low photon energy.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig06a.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig06b.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig06c.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig06d.pdf} \caption{The total energy $E_\mathrm{tot}$ (a), the mean number of photons $N_\gamma$ (b), the orbital magnetization $M_o$ (c), and the spin magnetization $M_s$ (d) of the 2DEG in a unit cell as function of the photon energy $E_\gamma$ for different values of the dimensionless electron-photon coupling $g_\gamma$. $T = 1.0$ K, $N_\mathrm{e} = 3$, $pq = 2$, $M_0 = \mu^*_\mathrm{B}/L^2$.} \label{Yfirlit-pq2-Ne03}
elements.sent-2411-092: Signs of this phase change in the static ring array are seen in all the variables displayed in Fig.\ \ref{Yfirlit-pq2-Ne03 , though they are clearest in subfigure (d) for the spin magnetization $M_s$.}
elements.sent-2411-093: We have done a calculation for the lower magnetic flux of $pq = 1$ for 3 electrons.
elements.sent-2411-094: There, for all values of the photon energy, we find the system to be maximally spin polarized for low electron-photon coupling, but for all other values of higher coupling, only a single unpaired spin is found in each ring.
elements.sent-2411-095: These signs of a spin-phase transition in the static system open the question if any signs of it can be observed in the dynamic evolution of the system after an excitation.
elements.sent-2411-097: The excitation of the array of quantum rings should not promote a change in the mean spin configuration.
elements.sent-2411-098: It can change the mean total orbital momentum, or the orbital magnetization in each ring with the induced circular electric field, but the spin should be conserved, but the mean spin in each ring might fluctuate around its original value.
elements.sent-2411-099: This is indeed what happens as can be seen in Fig.\ \ref{Yt-pq2-Ne03-gg008 for $g_\gamma = 0.08$.}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig07a.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig07b.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig07c.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig07d.pdf} \caption{The total energy $E_\mathrm{tot}$ (a), the mean number of photons $N_\gamma$ (b), the orbital magnetization $M_o$ (c), and the spin magnetization $M_s$ (d) of the 2DEG in a unit cell as function of time for the first 30 ps for different values of the photon energy $E_\gamma$. $g_\gamma = 0.08$, $N_\mathrm{e} = 3$, $pq = 2$, and $M_0 = \mu^*_\mathrm{B}/L^2$.} \label{Yt-pq2-Ne03-gg008}
elements.sent-2411-100: Additionally, we see a slight trend to a period doubling in the orbital magnetization, reflecting an increase of the higher order transitions being activated.
elements.sent-2411-101: The same behavior with more extremes can be seen for the case of the stronger electron-photon coupling $g_\gamma = 0.10$ displayed in Fig.\ \ref{Yt-pq2-Ne03-gg010 .}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig08a.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig08b.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig08c.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig08d.pdf} \caption{The total energy $E_\mathrm{tot}$ (a), the mean number of photons $N_\gamma$ (b), the orbital magnetization $M_o$ (c), and the spin magnetization $M_s$ (d) of the 2DEG in a unit cell as function of time for the first 30 ps for different values of the photon energy $E_\gamma$. $g_\gamma = 0.10$, $N_\mathrm{e} = 3$, $pq = 2$, and $M_0 = \mu^*_\mathrm{B}/L^2$.} \label{Yt-pq2-Ne03-gg010}
elements.sent-2411-102: In Fig.\ \ref{Ms-pq2-Ne03-gg this is compared for the total energy and the spin-magnetization for two different values of the photon energy, and three values of the electron-photon coupling.}
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig09a.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig09b.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig09c.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig09d.pdf} \caption{The total energy $E_\mathrm{tot}$ for the photon energy $E_\gamma = 0.70$ meV (a), $E_\gamma = 1.40$ meV (b), and the spin magnetization $M_s$ for $E_\gamma = 0.70$ meV (c), $E_\gamma = 1.40$ (d) of the 2DEG in a unit cell as function of time for the first 30 ps for different values of the electron-photon coupling $g_\gamma$. $N_\mathrm{e} = 3$, $pq = 2$, and $M_0 = \mu^*_\mathrm{B}/L^2$.} \label{Ms-pq2-Ne03-gg}
elements.sent-2411-103: It is particularly interesting to see the difference in the spin-magnetization in subfigure (c).
elements.sent-2411-104: For the low electron-photon coupling of $g_\gamma = 0.06$ no fluctuations are seen on the scale used to capture the fluctuations for the other values of the coupling.
elements.sent-2411-105: We stress that the fluctuations in the spin magnetization only show excitation peaks in the same energy locations as the mean photon number and the dynamic orbital magnetization.
elements.sent-2411-106: This reflects the fact that the chosen excitation can not exert any torque on the spins.
elements.sent-2411-107: We specially note that we do not observe any formation of a spin density wave in the angular direction within one quantum ring, neither in the static nor in the dynamic system.
elements.sent-2411-108: The total energy in Fig.\ \ref{Yt-pq2-Ne03-gg008 (a), \ref{Yt-pq2-Ne03-gg010 (a), and \ref{Ms-pq2-Ne03-gg (a) and (b) is not totally constant after the excitation pulse ends at $t = 16$ ps.}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: This has to be understood in relation with the Heisenberg uncertainty laws.
elements.sent-2411-110: The average of the total energy oscillates around a constant value.
elements.sent-2411-111: The number of electrons within a unit cell of the periodic system and the trace of the density matrices in each point $\bm{\theta}$ retain their correct values with 11 to 12 significant digits.
elements.sent-2411-112: We have used a QED-DFT-TP formalism to describe the Coulomb interaction and a CI approach for both the para- and the diamagnetic electron-photon interactions for a 2DEG in a large superlattice of quantum rings embedded in a cylindrical FIR-photon cavity in an external magnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-113: We find that in the ring array the Coulomb exchange interaction is stronger than in the corresponding array of quantum dots.
elements.sent-2411-114: For two electrons in each dot we observe a spin triplet state in each ring, even at low magnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-115: For three electrons and two magnetic flux quanta $pq = 2$ in each ring cell we find a fully spin polarized state for the static system for low electron-photon coupling and small photon energy.
elements.sent-2411-116: As the electron-photon coupling, or the photon energy, are increased a phase transition to a single unpaired spin in each cell is observed in the superlattice.
elements.sent-2411-117: For a single magnetic flux quantum $pq = 1$ through the unit cell we observe the same kind of a spin-phase transition with respect to the strength of the electron-photon coupling, whereas a change in the photon energy in the range we use does not lead to a phase transition.
elements.sent-2411-118: In the system with $pq = 2$ and two electrons in each ring a dynamical excitation of the system does not change its spin configuration, but tiny oscillations in the mean spin magnetization, most often just close to the numerical noise level, can be observed.
elements.sent-2411-119: The same could be said for all our former calculations for the excitation of arrays of quantum dots within the same formalism.
elements.sent-2411-120: The excitation does not affect the spin degree of freedom as analytical evaluations show, but only the population of different states.
elements.sent-2411-121: For the 3-electron rings in the $pq = 2$ magnetic flux, we observe spin fluctuations in the system close to a phase transition, even though the mean spin value is conserved.
elements.sent-2411-122: We observe thus, directly a spin-phase transition in the static system, but in the dynamical system we see the fluctuations that are known to be present in systems close to phase transitions.
elements.sent-2411-123: Both these phenomena support the idea that the superlattice, or array, of rings shows in this case a genuine spin-phase transition, and that the inter-ring exchange interaction is at work.
elements.sent-2411-124: Furthermore, the spin-phase transition can be suppressed by a strong electron-photon interaction.
elements.sent-2411-125: This suppression of the Coulomb exchange has been found previously in different models of corresponding quantum dots arrays.
elements.sent-2411-126: Finally, let us point out that the arrays of quantum dots or rings placed in photon cavities are in fact large and extended hybrid quantum systems, in contrast to smaller {\it qubit-like devices (e.g.\ quantum dots coupled to microwave cavities \cite{PhysRevX.7.011030,PhysRevX.12.031004 or color centers coupled by phononic modes \cite{PhysRevLett.132.263602,PhysRevX.9.031045 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-127: The latter are extensively used in quantum technologies, whereas the former provide important platforms merging collective effects and strong field-matter interaction.
elements.sent-2411-128: Note also that for large hybrid systems the theoretical tools and modeling are quite involved, as already illustrated in our present and previous studies based on QED-DFT and QED-DFT-TP methods.

elements.sent-2411-138: For the calculations we use all the same parameters as is detailed in Appendix B in Ref.\ \cite{PhysRevB.110.205301 , except that in Figures \ref{Yfirlit-pq2-Ne02 , \ref{d-pq2-Ne03 , and \ref{Yfirlit-pq2-Ne03 , we use the 16 lowest eigenstates of the photon number operator and a $32\times 32$ nonequispaced grid in the first Brillouin zone of the reciprocal space, $\bm{\theta}$, built on a repeated 4-point Gaussian quadrature.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: This is done to guarantee the accuracy of the results for the static system for the electron-photon coupling $g_\gamma > 0.10$.
elements.sent-2411-140: The ground state, or static, calculations are done in a linear functional basis constructed as a tensor product (TP) of electron and photon states $|\bm{\alpha\theta}\sigma n\rangle = |\bm{\alpha\theta}\sigma\rangle\otimes|n\rangle$.
elements.sent-2411-141: The electron states are states proposed by Ferrari \cite{Ferrari90:4598 , and used by Gudmundsson \cite{Gudmundsson95:16744 and Silberbauer \cite{Silberbauer92:7355 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-142: The photon states are the eigenstates of the photon number operator $N_\gamma = a^\dagger_\gamma a_\gamma$ with eigenvalue $n$.
elements.sent-2411-143: The self-consistent diagonalization of the static total Hamiltonian leads to the states $|\bm{\alpha\theta}\sigma n)$, but the Liouville-von Neumann Eq.\ (\ref{L-vN ) is solved in the $\{|\bm{\alpha\theta}\sigma n\rangle\}$-basis as most matrix elements are known analytically there \cite{Gudmundsson95:16744,PhysRevB.110.205301 .}}
